message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Rationale</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19-associated pulmonary aspergillosis (CAPA) is a life-threatening complication in patients with severe COVID-19. Previously, acute respiratory distress syndrome in patients with COVID-19 has been associated with lung fungal dysbiosis, evidenced by reduced microbial diversity and
                    <jats:italic>Candida</jats:italic>
                    colonisation. Increased fungal burden in the lungs of critically ill COVID-19 patients is linked to prolonged mechanical ventilation and increased mortality. However, specific mycobiome signatures associated with severe COVID-19 in the context of survival and antifungal drug prophylaxis have not yet been determined and such knowledge could have an important impact on treatment.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To understand the composition of the respiratory mycobiome in critically ill COVID-19 patients with and without CAPA and the impact of antifungal use in patient outcome.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We performed a multi-national study of 39 COVID-19 patients in intensive care units (ICU) with and without CAPA. Respiratory mycobiome was profiled using ITS1 sequencing and
                    <jats:italic>Aspergillus fumigatus</jats:italic>
                    burden was further validated using qPCR. Fungal communities were investigated using alpha diversity, beta diversity, taxa predominance and taxa abundances.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Respiratory mycobiomes of COVID-19 patients were dominated by
                    <jats:italic>Candida</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>Aspergillus.</jats:italic>
                    There was no significant association with corticosteroid use or CAPA diagnosis and respiratory fungal communities. Increased
                    <jats:italic>A. fumigatus</jats:italic>
                    burden was associated with mortality and, the use of azoles at ICU admission was linked with an absence of
                    <jats:italic>A. fumigatus</jats:italic>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Our findings suggest that mould-active antifungal treatment at ICU admission may be linked with reduced
                    <jats:italic>A. fumigatus-</jats:italic>
                    associated mortality in severe COVID-19. However, further studies are warranted on this topic.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Mycobiome analyses of critically ill COVID-19 patients
